text: young male patient presented to a hospital with a complaint of right hypochondriac pain intermittent which was and with jaundice for seven to ten days wvc counts were eighteen thousand and bilbin was ten point fifty nine and he had raised liver enzymes ultrasound suggested a mirage syndrome ct scan was done and that showed a conglomerated collection on the valvular fossa with the intrahepatic gb with the dilated cbd MRI with MRCP was done to confirm the findings which suggested almost the same findings In view of current finding in cholangitis ERCP was done and stent was placed

Upon opening the abdomen, dense adhesions are observed between the first part of the duodenum and the inferior surface of the gallbladder. The gallbladder is not visualized initially. Adhesions on the right side of the colon and right lobe of the liver are removed using monopolar diathermy. Dissection is performed flush to the liver surface, meticulously separating the duodenum from the underlying bile duct and inferior liver surface. A dilated bile duct with surrounding lymph nodes is visible. The duodenum is progressively separated from the bile duct, revealing the dissection plane. The gallbladder remains unvisualized, possibly indicating Mirizzi syndrome, with a potential connection between the bile duct and Hartman's pouch.

The surgeon explores the bile duct, making a puncture with diathermy. Upon opening the duct, bile is visible along with an endobiliary stent and a large stone near the stent. The stone is extracted, revealing the bile duct with the stent and the opening of a probable gallbladder contributing to Mirizzi syndrome on the right side. An intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) is planned to confirm the presence of a stone in an intrahepatic location.

An infant feeding tube is inserted into the suspected area on the right side, and contrast is injected, revealing a filling defect. The entire right biliary system fills, indicating a possible communication with the bile duct. The surgeon explores the area and performs a vertical incision on the interior wall of the collection, potentially a gallbladder or hepatotomy.

Using monopolar diathermy, the surgeon opens a cavity containing granulation tissue and a large stone. The stone is extracted and placed in Morrison's area. After washing and lavaging the cavity, the clear opening of the right hepatic duct is visible. A stent is placed in the duct to stabilize the system.

The surgeon secures the previously placed stent in the right hepatic ductal system and positions another endobiliary stent in the bile duct on the left side. Using polygalactic 2-0 suture material, multiple figure-of-eight sutures are applied to close the biliary tree defect over the stents, ensuring no stricture or narrowing occurs in the dilated common bile duct.

The surgeon sutures the Hartmann's pouch or biliary area, followed by closing the hepatotomy cavity with interrupted sutures to minimize biliary leakage. Saline irrigation is performed, and a flat drain is placed in Morrison's area. Stones are stored in a specimen retrieval bag, and the omentum is positioned over the drain. The surgery concludes successfully.
